title: Cisco ISE Certificate Expiry and Trust-Chain Health

compliance.assurance_rationale: PCI DSS 4.2 requires PAN protection during transmission over open or public networks via strong cryptography. ISE EAP-TLS and admin certificates are part of that cryptographic perimeter for cardholder-data environment access. Expiry monitoring evidences operational maintenance of the cryptographic boundary.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that cryptographic certificates protecting transmission to/from the access-control plane are maintained current and operational.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-17.1.31' alert log + monthly cert-inventory snapshot retained in audit_evidence index for the PCI annual cycle.

compliance.assurance_rationale: PCI DSS 12.3.3 requires cryptographic cipher suites and protocols in use be reviewed at least once every 12 months including documented change-management. Cert inventory + algorithm flagging supports the inventory side of this control.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence of an inventory of cryptographic primitives in use (certs, key sizes, signature algorithms) on the access-control plane to support the annual review.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Annual cert-inventory export (key size, signature algorithm) cross-referenced to the cryptographic-review register.

compliance.assurance_rationale: Art.21(2)(h) requires policies on cryptography. Certificate inventory + expiry monitoring evidences operational maintenance of cryptographic primitives on the access-control plane.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence operational maintenance of cryptographic certificates supporting NIS2-mandated access-control.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-17.1.31' alert log + cert-inventory monthly snapshot.

compliance.assurance_rationale: SC-12 (Cryptographic Key Establishment and Management) requires processes for cryptographic key management — establishment, rotation, revocation. Certificate-expiry monitoring is the rotation-detection arm of that process for the access-control plane.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence operational rotation/expiry handling of cryptographic certificates on the access-control plane.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: UC-17.1.31 alert log + cert-inventory feed retained 1y.

compliance.assurance_rationale: SC-17 (Public Key Infrastructure Certificates) requires the system issue/manage PKI certificates. Cert-inventory monitoring covers the management side.
compliance.controlObjective: Provide visibility into PKI certificates in use on the access-control plane for SC-17 management.

compliance.assurance_rationale: A.8.24 (Use of cryptography) requires policies for the use, lifecycle, and management of cryptographic keys. Cert-expiry monitoring is an operational implementation of the lifecycle component.
compliance.controlObjective: Operationalise cryptographic-key lifecycle for the access-control plane.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: UC-17.1.31 alert log + lifecycle entry in cryptographic-key-management register.

compliance.assurance_rationale: 164.312(e)(1) Transmission Security requires technical security measures to guard against unauthorised access to ePHI transmitted over a network. Cert maintenance on the access-control plane is foundational.
compliance.controlObjective: Maintain transmission-security cryptographic primitives on the access-control plane that enforces ePHI access.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: UC-17.1.31 alert log + cert-inventory snapshot retained per HIPAA 6y minimum.

controlTest.positiveScenario: On a lab ISE, generate a self-signed system certificate with a `validity = 30 days` setting. The 06:00 UTC daily run of UC-17.1.31 must list that cert with `severity=MEDIUM` and `days_left ≤ 30`. After ~16 days the severity must escalate to HIGH, and after 23 days to CRITICAL.
controlTest.negativeScenario: Renew the same certificate to a new validity of 365 days. The next 06:00 UTC run must NOT include this cert in the alert output (`days_left > 60` so the where-clause excludes it).

dataSources: Two-pronged collection. (1) ISE syslog via `index=nac sourcetype=cisco:ise:syslog` from Splunkbase 1915. Certificate-related codes appear under the *Administrative and Operational Audit* category — message codes `52050` (cert imported), `52051` (cert exported), `52052` (cert deleted), `52053` (CSR generated), `52054` (cert renewed), `52055` (CRL refresh failed), `52056` (OCSP responder unreachable), and the daily warnings `60520` (cert expiring within 90/60/30/14/7 days, severity escalates). (2) For comprehensive certificate inventory beyond the syslog snapshot, the Cisco ISE OpenAPI endpoint `/api/v1/trusted-certificate` and `/api/v1/system-certificate` returns the full cert store as JSON. A modular input on a Heavy Forwarder polls these every 6 hours and writes to `index=nac sourcetype=cisco:ise:cert_inventory`. Key fields: `subject`, `issuer`, `valid_from`, `valid_to`, `usage` (system|trust|admin|portal|saml|pxgrid|eap), `node`, `key_size`, `signature_algorithm`. CIM mapping: `Certificates.SSL` (if you use the Splunk SSL data model) — most teams treat this as bespoke and store it outside CIM.
app: `Splunk Add-on for Cisco Identity Services` ([Splunkbase 1915](https://splunkbase.splunk.com/app/1915)) for syslog. Custom modular input or scheduled `curl | collect` script for the OpenAPI cert inventory feed (provided in implementation below).
spl:
(index=nac sourcetype="cisco:ise:syslog" MessageCode IN (52055, 52056, 60520)) OR (index=nac sourcetype="cisco:ise:cert_inventory")
| eval days_until_expiry = round((strptime(valid_to, "%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%S") - now()) / 86400, 0)
| where (sourcetype="cisco:ise:cert_inventory" AND days_until_expiry < 60) OR (sourcetype="cisco:ise:syslog")
| eval severity = case(
    days_until_expiry < 7, "CRITICAL",
    days_until_expiry < 14, "HIGH",
    days_until_expiry < 30, "MEDIUM",
    days_until_expiry < 60, "LOW",
    MessageCode=="52055" OR MessageCode=="52056", "HIGH",
    true(), "INFO")
| stats min(days_until_expiry) as days_left, latest(valid_to) as expires, latest(usage) as usage, latest(severity) as severity, latest(_time) as last_seen by node, subject, issuer
| where severity != "INFO"
| sort + days_left
description: Detects Cisco ISE certificates that are within 60 days of expiry, certificates whose CRL refresh has failed, and OCSP responders that are unreachable. Covers system certificates (used for admin UI, EAP, pxGrid, portal, SAML), trusted certificates (CA chain), and warns on weak algorithms (SHA-1) and short keys (<2048 bits) flagged during the inventory polling.
value: An expired EAP system certificate on a PSN means every wireless and wired 802.1X authentication using that PSN as Authentication Server fails — that's tens of thousands of clients on the floor unable to log in, with the root cause buried in TLS handshake error logs. An expired admin-UI certificate means admins can't log in to the PAN to remediate. An expired pxGrid certificate cuts the SOC's context feed. This UC catches all of these 60 days out — enough time to procure a CA-signed cert and schedule the rollover during a maintenance window.
implementation: Two collections must be active. (1) Confirm the *Administrative and Operational Audit* logging category is enabled at INFO and forwarded to Splunk (UC-17.1.28's plumbing covers this). (2) Deploy the OpenAPI poller — a Splunk modular input or a cron-driven Python script on a Heavy Forwarder — that queries `/api/v1/system-certificate` and `/api/v1/trusted-certificate` every 6 hours, normalises into the cert_inventory sourcetype, and writes to `index=nac`. Schedule the alert daily at 06:00 (UTC), trigger on count > 0, throttle suppression on `node`+`subject` for 7 days so the same expiring cert generates one ticket per week, not one per day.
visualization: (1) Single value: "Certs expiring <7d" (red), "<14d" (red), "<30d" (orange), "<60d" (yellow). (2) Sortable table: node | usage | subject | issuer | days_left | severity. Drilldown opens a panel with the cert PEM (if your inventory feed includes the raw certificate). (3) Calendar heatmap: cert expirations over the next 12 months as a calendar — reveals month with cert-renewal cluster. Useful for staffing renewal work. (4) Bar chart: count of certs by issuer (last 1y inventory snapshot) — shows the distribution between internal CA and public CA, useful for audit.
dataModelAcceleration: Not applicable — cert inventory is a small dataset (<10k events/day for even large deployments) and the syslog signals are sparse. DMA gain is negligible.

references.title: Cisco ISE 3.4 Admin Guide — Certificate Management
Cisco ISE 3.4 Admin Guide — pxGrid Certificate Management
Splunk Add-on for Cisco Identity Services (Splunkbase 1915)

knownFalsePositives: (a) **Self-signed default certs not yet replaced** — fresh ISE deployments ship with a 1-year self-signed system cert that triggers the alert almost immediately (the deployment is brand-new). Maintain a `cert_exclusions.csv` lookup with `(node, subject, expected_replacement_date)` to suppress until the install team replaces with a CA-signed cert. (b) **CRL refresh failures during a transient internet outage** — single 52055 events that don't recur are not worth paging on. Only alert when 52055 occurs >3 times in 1h, indicating a sustained CA reachability issue. (c) **Internal CA's intermediate cert renewal** — when your internal PKI rolls a sub-CA, every ISE node needs the new chain pushed. The trusted-certificate inventory will show the old chain expiring in <30 days, but the renewal is in flight. Add an exclusion entry with the renewal change ticket. (d) **Test deployments with expired EAP certs** — labs and PoC deployments routinely run with expired certs. Tag those nodes with `environment=lab` in `ise_deployment.csv` and exclude with `lookup ise_deployment.csv node OUTPUT environment | where environment != "lab"`.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• ISE 3.0+ (the OpenAPI is present 3.0+; for 2.x the older ERS API at `/ers/config/certificate` is the path — same fields, different URL).
• An ERS / OpenAPI service account on ISE with `Read-only Admin` role for the certificate API. Configure under Administration > System > Admin Access > Administrators > Admin Users.
• Splunk credentials store: store the ERS user's password in the Splunk credentials manager (`splunk show passwd`-equivalent) — do NOT hardcode. Reference: the password-protected store under `$SPLUNK_HOME/etc/apps/<app>/local/passwords.conf`.
• `cert_exclusions.csv` lookup with columns (`node`, `subject`, `expected_replacement_date`, `change_ticket`).
• `ise_deployment.csv` lookup includes an `environment` column (lab/staging/prod).

Step 1 — Configure data collection
(a) Syslog signals — already covered by UC-17.1.28's Logging Categories config. Verify `Administrative and Operational Audit` at INFO is sending 52050/52055 events:
```spl
index=nac sourcetype="cisco:ise:syslog" MessageCode IN (52050, 52051, 52052, 52053, 52054, 52055, 52056, 60520) earliest=-30d
| stats count by MessageCode
```

(b) OpenAPI cert inventory poller. Two implementations are practical:

Option 1 — Splunk modular input via the `Splunk Add-on for REST API Modular Input` ([Splunkbase 1546](https://splunkbase.splunk.com/app/1546)). Define a stanza:
```ini
[rest://ise_system_cert]
endpoint = https://<ise-pan>:443/api/v1/system-certificate
auth_type = basic
username = ers_readonly
password = ${SECRET}
polling_interval = 21600
response_type = json
index = nac
sourcetype = cisco:ise:cert_inventory
response_handler = JsonResponseHandler
verify_ssl = 1
```
Replicate for `/api/v1/trusted-certificate` and for every PSN (system certs are per-node).

Option 2 — A cron-driven Python script on a Heavy Forwarder. The script:
1. Reads ISE base URL + credentials from a credentials file (mode 0600, owned by splunkfwd:splunkfwd).
2. Issues `GET /api/v1/system-certificate` and `/api/v1/trusted-certificate` for every node.
3. Normalises each cert into a JSON event with the `subject`, `issuer`, `valid_from`, `valid_to`, `usage`, `node`, `key_size`, `signature_algorithm` fields.
4. Appends to a file monitored by `inputs.conf`:
```ini
[monitor:///var/log/ise-cert-inventory.log]
index = nac
sourcetype = cisco:ise:cert_inventory
```
The Python script is one of these implementations; use whichever your team supports.

Validate the feed:
```spl
index=nac sourcetype="cisco:ise:cert_inventory" earliest=-1d
| stats count latest(valid_to) by node, subject, usage
```
For a 3-PSN deployment with system + trust + admin certs, expect ~50–200 cert rows per polling run.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Primary search:

```spl
(index=nac sourcetype="cisco:ise:syslog" MessageCode IN (52055, 52056, 60520))
  OR (index=nac sourcetype="cisco:ise:cert_inventory")
| eval days_until_expiry = round((strptime(valid_to, "%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%S") - now()) / 86400, 0)
| where (sourcetype="cisco:ise:cert_inventory" AND days_until_expiry < 60) OR (sourcetype="cisco:ise:syslog")
| eval severity = case(
    days_until_expiry < 7, "CRITICAL",
    days_until_expiry < 14, "HIGH",
    days_until_expiry < 30, "MEDIUM",
    days_until_expiry < 60, "LOW",
    MessageCode=="52055" OR MessageCode=="52056", "HIGH",
    true(), "INFO")
| lookup cert_exclusions.csv node, subject OUTPUT expected_replacement_date
| where isnull(expected_replacement_date) OR strptime(expected_replacement_date, "%Y-%m-%d") < now()
| lookup ise_deployment.csv node OUTPUT environment
| eval environment = coalesce(environment, "prod")
| where environment != "lab"
| stats min(days_until_expiry) as days_left, latest(valid_to) as expires, latest(usage) as usage, latest(severity) as severity, latest(_time) as last_seen by node, subject, issuer
| where severity != "INFO"
| sort + days_left
```

Schedule: cron `0 6 * * *`, time range `-1d@d to @d` (cert inventory feed), trigger on `count > 0`, throttle suppression on `node`+`subject` for 7 days.

Step 3 — Validate
(a) Review every (node, subject) row in the alert. Confirm the cert is genuinely expiring and either (i) generate a new CSR via the PAN UI or (ii) plan the rollover into the next cert-renewal change window.

(b) Field validation:
```spl
index=nac sourcetype="cisco:ise:cert_inventory" earliest=-7d
| stats values(usage) by node
```
Every PSN should have at least: `system`, `eap`, `admin`, `portal`. PANs should have: `system`, `admin`. pxGrid nodes: `pxgrid`. If a usage is missing, the cert binding may have been lost during a recent deployment change.

(c) Algorithm hygiene:
```spl
index=nac sourcetype="cisco:ise:cert_inventory" earliest=-1d
| stats count by signature_algorithm, key_size
```
Flag any SHA-1 signed certs (deprecate aggressively) and any RSA <2048-bit. PCI DSS 4.0 explicitly excludes SHA-1 in cryptographic primitives — you cannot pass an audit with SHA-1 system certs.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Dashboard "ISE — Certificate Lifecycle":
• Single value tiles: certs expiring <7/14/30/60d.
• Calendar heatmap: 12-month window of expiry distribution. Click a month → drill to the certs expiring in it.
• Sortable table: node | subject | issuer | usage | days_left | environment | replacement_ticket.
• Bar chart: cert count by issuer — distinguishes internal CA from public CA.
• Algorithm hygiene panel: count of SHA-1 / RSA<2048 / EC<P-256 certs.

Alerting:
• PagerDuty: high-urgency on CRITICAL (≤7d), low-urgency on HIGH (≤14d), no page on MEDIUM/LOW (table-only).
• Daily Slack/email digest: list of all certs in the table, grouped by month of expiry. Owner: ISE platform team + PKI team.
• Auto-create renewal tickets for any cert in MEDIUM/HIGH/CRITICAL via Splunk SOAR or webhook to your ticketing system.

Runbook (owner: ISE platform team + PKI team):
1. Confirm the cert is genuinely expiring (cross-check on the PAN UI: Administration > System > Certificates > System Certificates / Trusted Certificates).
2. Generate CSR. PAN UI: Administration > System > Certificates > Certificate Signing Requests > Generate CSR. Select the node, fill in the SAN field with all DNS names the cert needs to serve (PSN public hostname, EAP nickname, portal hostname).
3. Submit CSR to your CA (internal AD-CS or external public CA). For EAP-TLS server certs, document each SAN: most clients reject EAP server certs whose SAN doesn't match the connection name.
4. Receive signed cert + chain. Import on the PAN: Administration > System > Certificates > System Certificates > Import. Bind to usage (eap / admin / portal etc.).
5. Upon confirmation in the cert_inventory feed (next polling cycle), the alert clears.
6. For pxGrid: pxGrid clients (Splunk, Cisco SecureX, FMC, FTD) must trust the new pxGrid issuer. If you renewed the pxGrid system cert with the same issuer, no client work is needed. If you switched issuers, push the new chain to all clients before the cutover.

Step 5 — Troubleshooting

• **Cert renewed but alert keeps firing** — Polling cycle hasn't refreshed. The OpenAPI poller runs every 6h; the next run after the renewal will detect the new cert. Force a manual run if needed.

• **`days_until_expiry` shows negative numbers** — A cert has already expired. This is a P0 — verify the cert is actually in production use (not leftover in the inventory). If in use, the deployment is currently broken or about to break.

• **OpenAPI returns 401 on every poll** — The ERS user account is locked, password rotated, or account deleted. Reset and re-store credentials.

• **CRL refresh failures (52055) recur every hour** — The internal CA's CRL endpoint is unreachable from the ISE PSN. Test from the PSN CLI: `nslookup <crl-host>` and `curl <crl-url>`. If unreachable, fix the network path before the cached CRL ages out and EAP-TLS auths start failing.

• **OCSP unreachable (52056)** — Same as above but for the OCSP responder. Validate from the PSN CLI. Some deployments use OCSP stapling on the ISE node itself; check the PAN config: Administration > System > Certificates > OCSP Client Profiles.

• **Trusted-certificate inventory missing the new internal CA root** — Cisco ISE doesn't auto-import roots. The PKI team rolled the root in AD-CS, but the new root cert was never imported into ISE's Trusted Certificates store. Manually import via the UI before the old root expires.
grandmaExplanation: Every secure connection uses a digital ID card with an expiry date. We make a list of every ID card the access-control system uses and warn well in advance — 60, 30, 14, 7 days — before any expires, so renewal never sneaks up and breaks logins overnight.
